(Pods are\n52\t   generally Deployment-owned and not GitOps-managed, so a direct delete is a legitimate\n53\t   *operational* action — but it still must be deliberately targeted, not guessed.)\n54\t\n55\t5. **Destructive + reversible-via-recreation caveat.** If the target belongs to a\n56\t   Deployment/ReplicaSet it will simply respawn. That makes a guessed delete pointless\n57\t   *and* still potentially disruptive. Nine\nrecurrences make it definitive that the durable fix is **upstream**: the escalate\nparser/classifier should reject (or request) the missing operand *before* a bead is ever\ncreated. The repo's own parser already *requires* a `<pod-name>` token\n(`src/escalate/commands.py:92` `parse_delete_pod_utterance`, dispatched at\n`src/escalate/handler.py`) — so the bare command would already fail to parse there. The\nrecurrence is happening *before* that gate.\n\n### Why it cannot run as given\n\n1. **No pod name or selector.** `kubectl delete pod` with no argument is a no-op syntax\n   error; it needs a name (`my-pod-123`) or selector (`-l app=foo`). None was provided.\n\n2. **No cluster / no default context.** `kubectl config current-context` returns\n   `error: current-context is not set`. There is no ambient cluster in this shell —\n   access is via explicit per-cluster flags (proxies or kubeconfig paths). The task\n   names none.\n\n3. **Most clusters are read-only.** Per `CLAUDE.md`, every kubectl-proxy endpoint\n   (`traefik-*-manager`, `traefik-iad-options`, `kubectl-proxy-iad-kalshi`, …) carries\n   read-only RBAC — they **cannot delete**. Only `ardenone-manager` and `rs-manager`\n   have direct cluster-admin kubeconfigs, and `iad-ci` has a direct cluster-admin\n   kubeconfig. A blind delete would have to pick one of these.\n\n4. **GitOps policy.** Cluster writes are supposed to flow through\n   `jedarden/declarative-config` / ArgoCD, not direct `kubectl` mutations. (Pods are\n   generally Deployment-owned and not GitOps-managed, so a direct delete is a legitimate\n   *operational* action — but it still must be deliberately targeted, not guessed.)\n\n5. **Destructive + reversible-via-recreation caveat.** If the target belongs to a\n   Deployment/ReplicaSet it will simply respawn. That makes a guessed delete pointless\n   *and* still potentially disruptive. Confirming intent first is the right call.\n\n### Environment facts (re-verified 2026-07-19 on 9th re-release)\n\n- `kubectl config current-context` → `error: current-context is not set`\n- `~/.kube/` kubeconfigs actually on disk: `iad-acb.kubeconfig`, `iad-ci.kubeconfig`\n  (+ cache). CLAUDE.md also references `ardenone-manager.kubeconfig` /\n  `rs-manager.kubeconfig` / `iad-options.kubeconfig` — **not present on disk** here.\n- Escalate parser (`src/escalate/commands.py:92` `parse_delete_pod_utterance`) still\n  requires a `<pod-name>` operand via pattern `kubectl\\s+delete\\s+pod\\s+(\\S+)…`; a bare\n  `kubectl delete pod` raises `CommandExecutionError` (no operand to extract).\n\n### What's needed to proceed\n\nThe user must supply:\n1. **Pod name** (or `-l <selector>`) — required\n2. **Namespace** — if not the default for the chosen cluster\n3. **Cluster / kubeconfig** — e.g. one of:\n   - `~/.kube/iad-ci.kubeconfig` (cluster-admin, CI/CD)\n   - `ardenone-manager.kubeconfig` / `rs-manager.kubeconfig` (cluster-admin)\n   - a read-only proxy is *not* usable for delete\n\nOnce provided, the command is a one-liner:\n\n```bash\nkubectl --kubeconfig=<kubeconfig-path> delete pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>\n```\n\n### Escalate-system note\n\nThe repo already implements `kubectl delete pod` as an *escalate* intent\n(`src/escalate/commands.py` → `KubernetesCommandExecutor.execute_delete_pod`,\nauto-approved only in staging, manual approval via bead in production — see adc-5jl).\nA bare `kubectl delete pod` with no target does not map to that flow either, since the\nexecutor requires a parsed pod name + project_slug.\n\n## Resolution\nNot executed. Ten\\nrecurrences make it definitive that the durable fix is **upstream**: the escalate\\nparser/classifier should reject (or request) the missing operand *before* a bead is ever\\ncreated. The repo's own parser already *requires* a `<pod-name>` token\\n(`src/escalate/commands.py:92` `parse_delete_pod_utterance`, dispatched at\\n`src/escalate/handler.py`) — so the bare command would already fail to parse there. The\\nrecurrence is happening *before* that gate.\\n\\n### Why it cannot run as given\\n\\n1. **No pod name or selector.** `kubectl delete pod` with no argument is a no-op syntax\\n   error; it needs a name (`my-pod-123`) or selector (`-l app=foo`). None was provided.\\n\\n2. **No cluster / no default context.** `kubectl config current-context` returns\\n   `error: current-context is not set`. There is no ambient cluster in this shell —\\n   access is via explicit per-cluster flags (proxies or kubeconfig paths). The task\\n   names none.\\n\\n3. **Most clusters are read-only.** Per `CLAUDE.md`, every kubectl-proxy endpoint\\n   (`traefik-*-manager`, `traefik-iad-options`, `kubectl-proxy-iad-kalshi`, …) carries\\n   read-only RBAC — they **cannot delete**. Only `ardenone-manager` and `rs-manager`\\n   have direct cluster-admin kubeconfigs, and `iad-ci` has a direct cluster-admin\\n   kubeconfig. A blind delete would have to pick one of these.\\n\\n4. **GitOps policy.** Cluster writes are supposed to flow through\\n   `jedarden/declarative-config` / ArgoCD, not direct `kubectl` mutations. (Pods are\\n   generally Deployment-owned and not GitOps-managed, so a direct delete is a legitimate\\n   *operational* action — but it still must be deliberately targeted, not guessed.)\\n\\n5. **Destructive + reversible-via-recreation caveat.** If the target belongs to a\\n   Deployment/ReplicaSet it will simply respawn. That makes a guessed delete pointless\\n   *and* still potentially disruptive. Confirming intent first is the right call.\\n\\n### Environment facts (re-verified 2026-07-19 on 10th re-release)\\n\\n- `kubectl config current-context` → `error: current-context is not set`\\n- `~/.kube/` kubeconfigs actually on disk: `iad-acb.kubeconfig`, `iad-ci.kubeconfig`\\n  (+ cache). CLAUDE.md also references `ardenone-manager.kubeconfig` /\\n  `rs-manager.kubeconfig` / `iad-options.kubeconfig` — **not present on disk** here.\\n- Escalate parser (`src/escalate/commands.py:92` `parse_delete_pod_utterance`) still\\n  requires a `<pod-name>` operand via pattern `kubectl\\\\s+delete\\\\s+pod\\\\s+(\\\\S+)…`; a bare\\n  `kubectl delete pod` raises `CommandExecutionError` (no operand to extract).\\n\\n### What's needed to proceed\\n\\nThe user must supply:\\n1. **Pod name** (or `-l <selector>`) — required\\n2. **Namespace** — if not the default for the chosen cluster\\n3. **Cluster / kubeconfig** — e.g. one of:\\n   - `~/.kube/iad-ci.kubeconfig` (cluster-admin, CI/CD)\\n   - `ardenone-manager.kubeconfig` / `rs-manager.kubeconfig` (cluster-admin)\\n   - a read-only proxy is *not* usable for delete\\n\\nOnce provided, the command is a one-liner:\\n\\n```bash\\nkubectl --kubeconfig=<kubeconfig-path> delete pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>\\n```\\n\\n### Escalate-system note\\n\\nThe repo already implements `kubectl delete pod` as an *escalate* intent\\n(`src/escalate/commands.py` → `KubernetesCommandExecutor.execute_delete_pod`,\\nauto-approved only in staging, manual approval via bead in production — see adc-5jl).\\nA bare `kubectl delete pod` with no target does not map to that flow either, since the\\nexecutor requires a parsed pod name + project_slug.\\n\\n## Resolution\\nNot executed. No pod was deleted. No cluster was mutated. Task left open for the user\\nto specify a target.\\n\"}"}},"session_id":"b50b8177-2656-40e6-900c-488cc616bc9d","parent_tool_use_id":null,"uuid":"89dea551-2f7d-41bf-8e4e-bcd864687882"}

None was provided.\n\n2. **No cluster / no default context.** `kubectl config current-context` returns\n   `error: current-context is not set`. There is no ambient cluster in this shell —\n   access is via explicit per-cluster flags (proxies or kubeconfig paths). The task\n   names none.\n\n3. **Most clusters are read-only.** Per `CLAUDE.md`, every kubectl-proxy endpoint\n   (`traefik-*-manager`, `traefik-iad-options`, `kubectl-proxy-iad-kalshi`, …) carries\n   read-only RBAC — they **cannot delete**. Only `ardenone-manager` and `rs-manager`\n   have direct cluster-admin kubeconfigs, and `iad-ci` has a direct cluster-admin\n   kubeconfig. A blind delete would have to pick one of these.\n\n4. **GitOps policy.** Cluster writes are supposed to flow through\n   `jedarden/declarative-config` / ArgoCD, not direct `kubectl` mutations. (Pods are\n   generally Deployment-owned and not GitOps-managed, so a direct delete is a legitimate\n   *operational* action — but it still must be deliberately targeted, not guessed.)\n\n5. **Destructive + reversible-via-recreation caveat.** If the target belongs to a\n   Deployment/ReplicaSet it will simply respawn. That makes a guessed delete pointless\n   *and* still potentially disruptive. Confirming intent first is the right call.\n\n### Environment facts (re-verified 2026-07-19 on 10th re-release)\n\n- `kubectl config current-context` → `error: current-context is not set`\n- `~/.kube/` kubeconfigs actually on disk: `iad-acb.kubeconfig`, `iad-ci.kubeconfig`\n  (+ cache). CLAUDE.md also references `ardenone-manager.kubeconfig` /\n  `rs-manager.kubeconfig` / `iad-options.kubeconfig` — **not present on disk** here.\n- Escalate parser (`src/escalate/commands.py:92` `parse_delete_pod_utterance`) still\n  requires a `<pod-name>` operand via pattern `kubectl\\s+delete\\s+pod\\s+(\\S+)…`; a bare\n  `kubectl delete pod` raises `CommandExecutionError` (no operand to extract).\n\n### What's needed to proceed\n\nThe user must supply:\n1. **Pod name** (or `-l <selector>`) — required\n2. **Namespace** — if not the default for the chosen cluster\n3. **Cluster / kubeconfig** — e.g. one of:\n   - `~/.kube/iad-ci.kubeconfig` (cluster-admin, CI/CD)\n   - `ardenone-manager.kubeconfig` / `rs-manager.kubeconfig` (cluster-admin)\n   - a read-only proxy is *not* usable for delete\n\nOnce provided, the command is a one-liner:\n\n```bash\nkubectl --kubeconfig=<kubeconfig-path> delete pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>\n```\n\n### Escalate-system note\n\nThe repo already implements `kubectl delete pod` as an *escalate* intent\n(`src/escalate/commands.py` → `KubernetesCommandExecutor.execute_delete_pod`,\nauto-approved only in staging, manual approval via bead in production — see adc-5jl).\nA bare `kubectl delete pod` with no target does not map to that flow either, since the\nexecutor requires a parsed pod name + project_slug.\n\n## Resolution\nNot executed. The\nrecurrence is happening *before* that gate.\n\n### Why it cannot run as given\n\n1. **No pod name or selector.** `kubectl delete pod` with no argument is a no-op syntax\n   error; it needs a name (`my-pod-123`) or selector (`-l app=foo`). None was provided.\n\n2. **No cluster / no default context.** `kubectl config current-context` returns\n   `error: current-context is not set`. There is no ambient cluster in this shell —\n   access is via explicit per-cluster flags (proxies or kubeconfig paths). The task\n   names none.\n\n3. **Most clusters are read-only.** Per `CLAUDE.md`, every kubectl-proxy endpoint\n   (`traefik-*-manager`, `traefik-iad-options`, `kubectl-proxy-iad-kalshi`, …) carries\n   read-only RBAC — they **cannot delete**. Only `ardenone-manager` and `rs-manager`\n   have direct cluster-admin kubeconfigs, and `iad-ci` has a direct cluster-admin\n   kubeconfig. A blind delete would have to pick one of these.\n\n4. **GitOps policy.** Cluster writes are supposed to flow through\n   `jedarden/declarative-config` / ArgoCD, not direct `kubectl` mutations. (Pods are\n   generally Deployment-owned and not GitOps-managed, so a direct delete is a legitimate\n   *operational* action — but it still must be deliberately targeted, not guessed.)\n\n5. **Destructive + reversible-via-recreation caveat.** If the target belongs to a\n   Deployment/ReplicaSet it will simply respawn. That makes a guessed delete pointless\n   *and* still potentially disruptive. Confirming intent first is the right call.\n\n### Environment facts (re-verified 2026-07-19 on 10th re-release)\n\n- `kubectl config current-context` → `error: current-context is not set`\n- `~/.kube/` kubeconfigs actually on disk: `iad-acb.kubeconfig`, `iad-ci.kubeconfig`\n  (+ cache). CLAUDE.md also references `ardenone-manager.kubeconfig` /\n  `rs-manager.kubeconfig` / `iad-options.kubeconfig` — **not present on disk** here.\n- Escalate parser (`src/escalate/commands.py:92` `parse_delete_pod_utterance`) still\n  requires a `<pod-name>` operand via pattern `kubectl\\s+delete\\s+pod\\s+(\\S+)…`; a bare\n  `kubectl delete pod` raises `CommandExecutionError` (no operand to extract).\n\n### What's needed to proceed\n\nThe user must supply:\n1. **Pod name** (or `-l <selector>`) — required\n2. **Namespace** — if not the default for the chosen cluster\n3. **Cluster / kubeconfig** — e.g. one of:\n   - `~/.kube/iad-ci.kubeconfig` (cluster-admin, CI/CD)\n   - `ardenone-manager.kubeconfig` / `rs-manager.kubeconfig` (cluster-admin)\n   - a read-only proxy is *not* usable for delete\n\nOnce provided, the command is a one-liner:\n\n```bash\nkubectl --kubeconfig=<kubeconfig-path> delete pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>\n```\n\n### Escalate-system note\n\nThe repo already implements `kubectl delete pod` as an *escalate* intent\n(`src/escalate/commands.py` → `KubernetesCommandExecutor.execute_delete_pod`,\nauto-approved only in staging, manual approval via bead in production — see adc-5jl).\nA bare `kubectl delete pod` with no target does not map to that flow either, since the\nexecutor requires a parsed pod name + project_slug.\n\n## Resolution\nNot executed.
